questions.question: You would like to set a maximum number of concurrent jobs in Cloud Dataproc. How would you do that?
options: Set computengine:mgi.scheduler.max-concurrent-jobs property when creating a managed instance group for Cloud Dataproc cluster.
Set dataproc:dataproc.scheduler.max-concurrent-jobs property when creating a cluster.
Set dataproc:dataproc.scheduler.max-concurrent-jobs property when adding worker nodes.
Use Cloud Monitoring to detect the number of jobs running and when the maximum threshold is exceeded, trigger a Cloud Function to terminate the most recently created job.
answer: Set dataproc:dataproc.scheduler.max-concurrent-jobs property when creating a cluster.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is to set the dataproc:dataproc.scheduler.max-concurrent-jobs property when creating a cluster. That property is not set when adding worker nodes. Properties of a Dataproc cluster that are specific to Dataproc are not set in Compute Engine. You do not need to monitor and terminate jobs using ad hoc procedures like triggering a Cloud Function after a maximum threshold is exceeded. See https://cloud.google.com/dataproc/docs/concepts/configuring-clusters/cluster-properties

questions.question: How can you efficiently deploy a data processing application on Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE), ensuring that containers are launched with the latest available configurations from a container registry, while also ensuring that your GKE nodes have GPUs, local SSDs, and 8 Gbps bandwidth, and you can manage the deployment process?
options: Use gcloud commands and Compute Engine startup scripts to pull container images and provision the infrastructure
Use Cloud Build with Terraform build to provision the infrastructure and launch containers with the latest available configurations from the container registry
Use gcloud commands to provision the infrastructure and enable autoscaling of containers on GKE
Use Cloud Scheduler and Dataflow to provision the data pipeline and run the job.
answer: Use Cloud Build with Terraform build to provision the infrastructure and launch containers with the latest available configurations from the container registry
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. Use Cloud Build with Terraform build to provision the infrastructure and launch containers with the latest available configurations from the container registry. Using Cloud Build with Terraform build is a best practice for automating infrastructure deployment in a reproducible way. By using Terraform to define the infrastructure as code, you can ensure that your GKE nodes have GPUs, local SSDs, and 8 Gbps bandwidth, and that the latest available container images are used. Cloud Build can then be used to schedule a job that provisions the infrastructure and launches the containers, allowing for an efficient deployment process. Option A is incorrect because Compute Engine startup scripts are not the best way to provision infrastructure on GKE. Option C is incorrect because it does not take advantage of the benefits of using Terraform and Cloud Build. Option D is incorrect because Dataflow is not the appropriate tool for provisioning infrastructure on GKE.

questions.question: A financial institution plans to use Dialogflow to create a chatbot for their mobile app. Chat logs have been reviewed and customer requests have been tagged for intent. 70% of the requests are simple and require 10 or fewer intents, while the other 30% require more complex requests. The company wants to know which intents should be automated first.?
options: Automate the 10 intents that cover 70% of the requests so that live agents can handle more complicated requests
Automate the more complicated requests first because those require more of the agents‘ time
Automate a blend of the shortest and longest intents to represent all intents
Automate intents where common words such as ‘payment‘ appear only once so the software isn‘t confused
answer: Automate the 10 intents that cover 70% of the requests so that live agents can handle more complicated requests
questions.explanation: Automating the intents that cover the majority of customer requests will help reduce the workload for live agents, allowing them to focus on more complex requests. By automating these simpler requests first, the chatbot can provide a more efficient customer service experience overall.

questions.question: You need to automate the execution of a complex data pipeline on Google Cloud that involves multiple Dataproc and Dataflow jobs with interdependencies. The pipeline should run daily, and you prefer to use managed services wherever possible. Which tool should you use to automate the pipeline?
options: Cloud Functions
Cloud Composer
Cloud Scheduler
Dataproc Workflow Templates
questions.explanation: Cloud Composer is a fully managed workflow orchestration service that allows you to author, schedule, and monitor complex workflows on Google Cloud. It provides a managed Apache Airflow service that makes it easy to create and manage DAGs (Directed Acyclic Graphs) for your pipeline. With Cloud Composer, you can easily define and schedule your pipeline to run on a daily basis, while also leveraging managed services such as Dataproc and Dataflow for processing. It also provides advanced features such as built-in monitoring, alerting, and logging to help you debug and optimize your pipeline.

questions.question: You have a Dataprep recipe that was created on a sample of data in a BigQuery table. How can you reuse this recipe on a daily basis for new data with the same schema, after a variable load job has completed?
options: Schedule a Dataprep job using a cron schedule
Use an App Engine cron job to schedule the execution of the Dataprep job
Export the recipe as a Dataprep template, and schedule a job using Cloud Scheduler
Export the Dataprep job as a Dataflow template, and incorporate it into a Cloud Composer job.
answer: Export the Dataprep job as a Dataflow template, and incorporate it into a Cloud Composer job.
questions.explanation: Correct Option D. The best approach is to export the Dataprep job as a Dataflow template and then orchestrate it with Cloud Composer (which is built on Apache Airflow). This allows the recipe to be reused daily on new data loads with the same schema. Cloud Composer provides workflow automation and scheduling, ensuring the Dataflow job runs after the variable load job completes. This is the most scalable and production-ready solution for recurring transformations in Google Cloud. Incorrect Option A. Dataprep does not natively support cron scheduling. While jobs can be scheduled, cron-like scheduling is not the recommended or integrated approach for production workflows. Option B. App Engine cron jobs are not designed to directly manage Dataprep executions. This would require custom integration and is not part of the baseline recommended architecture. Option C. Exporting the recipe as a Dataprep template and scheduling with Cloud Scheduler is not sufficient. Cloud Scheduler can trigger jobs, but Dataprep recipes are not directly executable in this way. They need to be converted into Dataflow templates for automation.

questions.question: You need to quickly and efficiently generate daily reports that show a customer‘s net consumption of Google Cloud compute resources and who used the resources. What should you do?
options: Export Cloud Logging data to BigQuery daily and create views that filter by project, log type, resource, and user
Filter data in Cloud Logging by project, resource, and user, then export the data in CSV format daily
Filter data in Cloud Logging by project, log type, resource, and user daily, then import the data into BigQuery
Export Cloud Logging data to Cloud Storage in CSV format daily, cleanse the data using Dataprep, and filter by project, resource, and user
answer: Export Cloud Logging data to BigQuery daily and create views that filter by project, log type, resource, and user
questions.explanation: The best approach to generating daily reports that show a customer‘s net consumption of Google Cloud compute resources and who used the resources is to do daily exports of Cloud Logging data to BigQuery and create views filtering by project, log type, resource, and user. This approach allows for quick and efficient generation of reports, as the data is already stored in BigQuery and can be easily queried and filtered to meet the specific requirements of the customer‘s report. Additionally, this approach enables easy automation of the report generation process using tools such as Cloud Scheduler and Cloud Functions.

questions.question: You used Cloud Dataprep to create a recipe on a sample of data in a BigQuery table. You want to reuse this recipe on a daily upload of data with the same schema, after the load job with variable execution time completes. What should you do?
options: Create a cron schedule in Cloud Dataprep.
Create an App Engine cron job to schedule the execution of the Cloud Dataprep job.
Export the recipe as a Cloud Dataprep template, and create a job in Cloud Scheduler.
Export the Cloud Dataprep job as a Cloud Dataflow template, and incorporate it into a Cloud Composer job.
answer: Export the Cloud Dataprep job as a Cloud Dataflow template, and incorporate it into a Cloud Composer job.
questions.explanation: Should be D https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/data-analytics/how-to-orchestrate-cloud-dataprep-jobs-using-cloud-composer Were happy to announce the latest release of Cloud Dataprep, which exposes orchestration APIs so you can integrate Cloud Dataprep within your schedulers or other orchestration solutions like Cloud Composer. This means you can expand your automation beyond Cloud Dataflow templates through direct integration in other tools to create repeatable data pipelines for your analytics and AI/ML initiativessaving time and adding reliability. In addition, this API lets you use dynamic inputs and outputs through Cloud Dataprep variables or parametersnot available using Cloud Dataflow templates. As a result, you can re-use a single Cloud Dataprep flow to execute on a range of input/output values that are evaluated at runtime.

questions.question: You want to automate execution of a multi-step data pipeline running on Google Cloud. The pipeline includes Cloud Dataproc and Cloud Dataflow jobs that have multiple dependencies on each other. You want to use managed services where possible, and the pipeline will run every day. Which tool should you use?
options: cron
Cloud Composer
Cloud Scheduler
Workflow Templates on Cloud Dataproc
questions.explanation: B: Cloud Composer is a fully managed workflow orchestration service that empowers you to author, schedule, and monitor pipelines that span across clouds and on-premises data centres. https://cloud.google.com/composer/

questions.question: Scenario: You have a data processing application that operates on Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE). The application requires launching containers with updated configurations from a container registry. Your GKE nodes must be equipped with GPUs, local SSDs, and 8 Gbps bandwidth. How can you effectively provision the data processing infrastructure and oversee the deployment process?
options: Use Compute Engine startup scripts to pull container images, and use gcloud commands to provision the infrastructure.
Use Cloud Build to schedule a job using Terraform build to provision the infrastructure and launch with the most current container images.
Use GKE to autoscale containers, and use gcloud commands to provision the infrastructure.
Use Dataflow to provision the data pipeline, and use Cloud Scheduler to run the job.
answer: Use Cloud Build to schedule a job using Terraform build to provision the infrastructure and launch with the most current container images.
questions.explanation: Option B is the correct answer for the given scenario.Using Cloud Build to schedule a job using Terraform will allow you to efficiently provision the data processing infrastructure and manage the deployment process. Terraform is an infrastructure as code tool that allows you to define and provision infrastructure in a declarative manner. By using Terraform in conjunction with Cloud Build, you can automate the provisioning of GKE nodes with GPUs, local SSDs, and 8 Gbps bandwidth, as well as launch containers with their latest available configurations from a container registry.Now, let‘s explain why the other options are incorrect:A. Using Compute Engine startup scripts to pull container images and using gcloud commands to provision the infrastructure is not the most efficient approach in this scenario. This method would require more manual intervention and scripting to achieve the desired outcome compared to using Terraform with Cloud Build.C. Using GKE to autoscale containers and using gcloud commands to provision the infrastructure might help with the scaling of containers, but it does not address the efficient provisioning of the required infrastructure components (GPUs, local SSDs, 8 Gbps bandwidth) and launching containers with the latest configurations from a container registry.D. Using Dataflow to provision the data pipeline and using Cloud Scheduler to run the job is not the most suitable approach for provisioning GKE nodes with GPUs, local SSDs, and 8 Gbps bandwidth, and launching containers with their latest configurations. Dataflow is more focused on data processing pipelines rather than infrastructure provisioning.

questions.question: Scenario: You are setting up a DAG in a Cloud Composer 2 instance where Apache Airflow processes incoming files from a Cloud Storage bucket one by one. The Cloud Composer instance is in a subnetwork without Internet access. You aim to trigger the DAG whenever a new file is received rather than based on a fixed schedule. How can you achieve running the DAG reactively each time a new file is added in this setup?
options: 1. Enable Private Google Access in the subnetwork, and set up Cloud Storage notifications to a Pub/Sub topic. 2. Create a push subscription that points to the web server URL.

1. Enable the Cloud Composer API, and set up Cloud Storage notifications to trigger a Cloud Function. 2. Write a Cloud Function instance to call the DAG by using the Cloud Composer API and the web server URL. 3. Use VPC Serverless Access to reach the web server URL.

1. Enable the Airflow REST API, and set up Cloud Storage notifications to trigger a Cloud Function instance. 2. Create a Private Service Connect (PSC) endpoint. 3. Write a Cloud Function that connects to the Cloud Composer cluster through the PSC endpoint.

1. Enable the Airflow REST API, and set up Cloud Storage notifications to trigger a Cloud Function instance. 2. Write a Cloud Function instance to call the DAG by using the Airflow REST API and the web server URL. 3. Use VPC Serverless Access to reach the web server URL.
answer: 1. Enable the Airflow REST API, and set up Cloud Storage notifications to trigger a Cloud Function instance. 2. Create a Private Service Connect (PSC) endpoint. 3. Write a Cloud Function that connects to the Cloud Composer cluster through the PSC endpoint.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is option C. Here‘s why:1. Enable the Airflow REST API: Enabling the Airflow REST API allows external services to trigger DAG runs in Apache Airflow, which is what you need in this scenario. When a new file is received in the Cloud Storage bucket, you can set up Cloud Storage notifications to trigger a Cloud Function that will interact with the Airflow REST API to start the DAG processing the new file.2. Create a Private Service Connect (PSC) endpoint: A Private Service Connect (PSC) endpoint allows private access to specific Google APIs and services. By creating a PSC endpoint, you ensure that the Cloud Function can securely connect to the Cloud Composer cluster without needing public internet access. This is essential since the Cloud Composer instance is deployed in a subnetwork with no internet access.3. Write a Cloud Function that connects to the Cloud Composer cluster through the PSC endpoint: The Cloud Function you write will use the PSC endpoint to communicate with the Airflow REST API of the Cloud Composer cluster. This setup ensures that the DAG is triggered reactively every time a new file is received in the Cloud Storage bucket.Now, let‘s explain why the other options are incorrect:– Option A suggests setting up Cloud Storage notifications to a Pub/Sub topic and creating a push subscription to a web server URL. This approach doesn‘t involve the Airflow REST API or Private Service Connect, which are crucial for secure communication with the Cloud Composer instance in a subnetwork with no internet access.– Option B involves triggering a Cloud Function through Cloud Storage notifications to call the DAG using the Cloud Composer API. However, this option doesn‘t mention the Airflow REST API, which is the recommended way to trigger DAGs in Apache Airflow. Additionally, VPC Serverless Access is not the ideal solution for securely communicating with the Cloud Composer cluster in a restricted subnetwork.– Option D is similar to Option B in that it suggests using the Airflow REST API to trigger the DAG through a Cloud Function. However, it also mentions VPC Serverless Access, which is not the most secure or appropriate method for connecting to the Cloud Composer cluster in a subnetwork without internet access.In summary, Option C is the correct choice because it leverages the Airflow REST API, Private Service Connect, and a Cloud Function to ensure secure and reactive triggering of the DAG in the Cloud Composer instance.

questions.question: Scenario: You have multiple Spark jobs scheduled to run on a Cloud Dataproc cluster. Some jobs run sequentially, while others run concurrently. How can you automate this process?
options: Create a Cloud Dataproc Workflow Template
Create an initialization action to execute the jobs
Create a Directed Acyclic Graph in Cloud Composer
Create a Bash script that uses the Cloud SDK to create a cluster, execute jobs, and then tear down the cluster
answer: Create a Directed Acyclic Graph in Cloud Composer
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C. Create a Directed Acyclic Graph in Cloud Composer.– Cloud Composer is a managed workflow orchestration service that allows you to author, schedule, and monitor workflows in the cloud. With Cloud Composer, you can create Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) to define the sequence and dependencies of your tasks or jobs.– By creating a DAG in Cloud Composer, you can automate the process of running Spark jobs on a Cloud Dataproc cluster, both in sequence and concurrently. You can define the order in which the jobs should run, as well as any dependencies between them.Why the other options are incorrect:A. Creating a Cloud Dataproc Workflow Template: While Cloud Dataproc supports the concept of workflow templates, they are more suitable for defining and running a sequence of jobs on a Dataproc cluster. However, they do not provide the flexibility and scheduling capabilities of a DAG in Cloud Composer.B. Creating an initialization action to execute the jobs: Initialization actions in Cloud Dataproc are scripts that run before the cluster starts and can be used to install additional software or set up configurations. They are not designed for orchestrating the execution of jobs in a specific sequence or concurrently.D. Creating a Bash script that uses the Cloud SDK: While it is possible to use the Cloud SDK to create a cluster, execute jobs, and tear down the cluster, this approach lacks the workflow automation and monitoring capabilities provided by Cloud Composer. Managing dependencies and scheduling jobs in a Bash script can be complex and error-prone compared to using a DAG in Cloud Composer.

questions.question: Scenario: You are employed by a major financial organization that intends to implement Dialogflow for a chatbot on the company‘s mobile application. After analyzing previous chat interactions, you have categorized each conversation by intent, based on the customer‘s purpose for reaching out to customer service. Approximately 70% of customer inquiries are basic and can be resolved within 10 intents, while the remaining 30% are more intricate and time-consuming. In prioritizing automation, which intents should be addressed first?
options: Automate the 10 intents that cover 70% of the requests so that live agents can handle more complicated requests.
Automate the more complicated requests first because those require more of the agents‘ time.
Automate a blend of the shortest and longest intents to be representative of all intents.
Automate intents in places where common words such as ‘payment‘ appear only once so the software isn‘t confused.
answer: Automate the 10 intents that cover 70% of the requests so that live agents can handle more complicated requests.
questions.explanation: A. Automate the 10 intents that cover 70% of the requests so that live agents can handle more complicated requests is the correct answer.Automating the 10 intents that cover 70% of the requests is the most efficient approach because it allows the chatbot to handle the majority of customer inquiries quickly and effectively. By automating these commonly occurring intents, live agents can then focus on handling the more complex and time-consuming requests that make up the remaining 30% of inquiries. This strategy helps to streamline the customer service process, improve response times, and enhance overall customer satisfaction.Why the other options are incorrect:B. Automate the more complicated requests first because those require more of the agents‘ time:This option is not the best approach because automating the more complicated requests first may not be efficient. It is more practical to automate the commonly occurring intents that cover the majority of requests to optimize the chatbot‘s performance and free up live agents to handle complex inquiries.C. Automate a blend of the shortest and longest intents to be representative of all intents:While it is important to consider a variety of intents when automating a chatbot, focusing on a blend of the shortest and longest intents may not be the most effective strategy. Prioritizing the intents that cover 70% of requests ensures that the chatbot can efficiently handle the most common customer inquiries.D. Automate intents in places where common words such as ‘payment‘ appear only once so the software isn‘t confused:Automating intents based on the occurrence of common words may not be the best approach as it does not guarantee that the chatbot will be able to effectively address customer requests. It is more beneficial to prioritize intents based on their frequency and relevance to customer needs to ensure a successful chatbot implementation.
